howItWorks.intro: This sea level rise calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Rise projection scaled linearly from 2020 baseline to 2100 target
NOAA scenarios: Low (1ft), Intermediate (2ft), High (4ft), Extreme (6.5ft) by 2100
Storm surge adds 3-15+ feet on top of sea level during hurricanes
howItWorks.outro: Energy costs and incentives change by region and season. Check with your local utility for current rates.

faq.question: How much will sea levels rise?
faq.answer: NOAA projects 1-6.5 feet of rise by 2100 depending on emissions. The current rate is ~3.6mm/year and accelerating. Even under best-case scenarios, at least 1 foot of rise is locked in from ice already melting.

faq.question: What areas are most at risk?
faq.answer: South Florida, Louisiana Gulf Coast, Chesapeake Bay, NYC metro, and Pacific islands face the highest risk. Any coastal area below 10 feet elevation should plan for eventual flooding impacts.

faq.question: Does this account for storm surge?
faq.answer: No — this shows baseline sea level only. Hurricane storm surge adds 3-15+ feet. A Category 3 hurricane with 3 feet of sea level rise could push water 18+ feet above current sea level in vulnerable areas.

howWeCalculate.intro: This sea level rise calculator uses established formulas and current data to provide accurate estimates.
steps: Enter your specific values into the calculator fields above
Our algorithm applies the relevant formulas using your inputs

howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These calculations are estimates based on standard formulas. For critical decisions, always consult a qualified professional.
